title: Quick weeknight lasagne
subtitle: Forty-five minutes from chopping board to table.
excerpt: A weeknight lasagne built around passata instead of a three-hour ragù. Browned mince, garlic, herbs, and tomato passata in twenty minutes; a quick all-in-one béchamel; four layers of dried sheets; thirty minutes in a hot oven. Feeds four.
sourceNotes: The structural reference is the slow version of lasagne alla bolognese (Artusi, 1891) shortened for a weeknight. The weeknight pattern of passata-based meat sauce and an all-in-one béchamel comes from the Anglo-American household-magazine tradition that adapted Italian lasagne to home kitchens in the mid-twentieth century.

content.text: A weeknight lasagne built around tomato passata rather than a three-hour ragù. Forty-five minutes from chopping board to table, dinner for four, the same comfort as the long version but in a different gear. The slow Bolognese earns its three hours on a Sunday; this earns its forty-five on a Tuesday.

The shortcuts hold up. Passata replaces the slow simmer of stock and wine; the meat sauce thickens in twenty minutes instead of three hours. The béchamel is built all-in-one: cold milk into a roux, then up to the boil whisking the whole time. Four layers of dried sheets, not six. The result is shallower and tomato-led where the proper one is deep and meaty, but it eats well and the kids like it.

attrs.body: Tomato passata is sieved raw tomato; it cooks down fast and brings instant savoury depth where a slow ragù relies on long reduction. The trade-off is brightness rather than darkness. A tablespoon of tomato purée stirred in alongside the passata gives the sauce a little more body and rounds the acidity. The kids' version skips the chilli flakes; the adults' keeps them.
attrs.title: Why passata works here

content.text: What you need

A wide sauté pan does the meat sauce in one go. The béchamel needs a small saucepan and a balloon whisk; a flat whisk works too. The bake dish should be deep enough to take four layers without the top sitting proud of the rim.
Method
Brown the mince

Heat the oven to 200°C fan, 220°C conventional. Warm {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide sauté pan over a medium heat. Tip in {{onion}} chopped onion and cook for five minutes until soft. Add {{garlic}} of crushed garlic and a pinch of chilli flakes if you want them; cook for thirty seconds. Add {{beef-mince}} of mince, breaking it up with a wooden spoon. Turn the heat up and brown the meat for eight minutes until it's lost its raw colour and the bottom of the pan starts to catch.
Build the sauce

Stir in {{tomato-puree}} of tomato purée and cook for a minute. Pour in {{tomato-passata}} of passata, add {{oregano-dried}} of dried oregano, and bring to a simmer. Turn the heat to low and let it bubble for ten minutes until the sauce thickens enough to coat the back of a spoon. Season with fine salt and freshly ground black pepper. Taste; passata varies wildly in salt content so adjust.
Make the all-in-one béchamel

Put {{unsalted-butter}} of butter, {{plain-flour}} of plain flour, and {{whole-milk}} of cold milk into a small saucepan. Whisk constantly over a medium heat until the butter melts and the sauce starts to thicken. Keep whisking; bring to a gentle boil. The flour cooks out and the sauce thickens to the texture of pourable custard in about six minutes. Add a pinch of ground nutmeg and a pinch of salt. Pull off the heat.
Assemble four layers

Spoon a thin layer of meat sauce across the base of the dish. Cover with a single layer of pasta sheets, breaking to fit. Spoon over a third of the remaining meat sauce, then a quarter of the béchamel, then a scatter of mozzarella. Repeat twice more: pasta, sauce, béchamel, mozzarella. Finish with a fourth layer of pasta, the last quarter of béchamel poured over the top, and the grated parmesan scattered evenly.
Bake

Slide the dish into the middle of the oven. Bake for twenty-five to thirty minutes until the top is deeply gold and the edges bubble. The pasta sheets should give to a knife slipped in at the centre; if there's resistance, give it five more minutes. Rest for ten minutes before slicing so the layers hold.

attrs.intro: The quick build is forgiving but trips at the same points the long version does: hard pasta, watery base, burnt top.

items.fix: Both meat sauce and béchamel should be pourable when you assemble. Loosen the meat sauce with a splash of water and the béchamel with a splash of milk if either has stiffened. No-pre-cook sheets need wetter sauces than the packet implies.
items.cause: Sauces too thick to wet the dried pasta through.
items.symptom: Pasta sheets still firm after baking.

items.fix: Simmer the meat sauce a few minutes longer next time so it sits on a spoon rather than running off. If using fresh mozzarella di bufala, drain and pat dry first; the firmer block mozzarella is drier and behaves better here.
items.cause: Meat sauce finished too wet, or mozzarella released a lot of water.
items.symptom: Watery puddle in the bottom of the dish.

items.fix: Drop to 180°C fan and slide the dish one rack lower. Cover loosely with foil for the first fifteen minutes, then uncover to brown.
items.cause: Oven too hot or dish too high in the oven.
items.symptom: Top burnt before the centre was cooked through.

items.fix: Ten minutes out of the oven before the first cut. Lasagne sets as it cools; that's what gives a clean square. Hot from the oven the layers slide past each other.
items.symptom: Slice slips apart on the plate.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting

content.text: Variations

A vegetarian version swaps the mince for diced courgette and aubergine, fried hard in olive oil before the onion goes in; the rest of the build holds. For a lighter dish, drop the béchamel and double the mozzarella, layering torn pieces between the sheets. The bake time stays the same.
Make ahead, freezing, leftovers

The meat sauce holds two days in the fridge. The assembled, unbaked lasagne keeps a day in the fridge under cling film; add ten minutes to the bake time if it goes in cold. Baked, the lasagne freezes well in portions for three months: reheat from frozen in a 160°C oven for forty minutes covered with foil, then ten minutes uncovered. Leftovers eat well cold the next day, sliced into the lunchbox.
Where this dish lives

The weeknight lasagne is an Anglo-American adaptation, not a true Italian dish. It comes from mid-1900s household magazines that took the Bolognese model and shortened every step to fit a weekday kitchen. Passata stands in for stock and wine. An all-in-one béchamel replaces the roux-and-warm-milk method. Mozzarella appears between the layers, where the orthodox version has none. The Italian-American baked-ziti tradition crossed into the British weeknight version through the 1970s and 1980s. That is why mozzarella ends up here.

The slow version remains the cornerstone for a Sunday or a dinner party; this one is for a Tuesday with two hungry children and a small window. Both are useful. They sit in different parts of the week.
